title: Quorum Queues and Replicated Delivery Claims · Language Lens: Replica Majority Safety
summary: A Go 1.26 service team must compute majority availability, leader disruption, poison limits, confirm evidence, and restore limits without overstating safety; reviewers reject hidden goroutine ownership, unbounded work, stale channel state, and claims that stop at a nil client error. The learner predicts values, ownership transitions, and stop paths before seeing a model. Planned artifact: Add and verify the replica majority safety increment in a Go quorum-availability and delivery-limit model.…
objectives: Derive tolerated failures and availability from odd replica count, online majority, leader election, placement, and maintenance sequence; in Go, retain ownership, stop-path, and changed-case evidence.

Choose quorum queues for durable replicated data safety and reject them for high-churn temporary queues, lowest latency, or extreme backlogs better served by streams; in Go, retain ownership, stop-path, and changed-case evidence.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

steps.title: RabbitMQ and Go Predict Replica Majority Safety · Theory Replica Majority Safety
steps.instruction:
theory case — A theory Go messaging team handles case 7513 during consumer shutdown drill with one blocked handler goroutine. Build deterministic Go evidence to compute majority availability while separating quorum custody from stakeholder effect; retain screen-reader-readable topology and delivery evidence; then fail the canary and prove older Go consumers remain compatible. Browser code uses pure values and state transitions only. amqp091-go, AMQP sockets, RabbitMQ, TLS, partitions, restarts, Docker, databases, race and leak checks, load, upgrades, and production behavior require explicit authorized transfer gates. Competency probe: Derive tolerated failures and availability from odd replica count, online majority, leader election, placement, and maintenance sequence; in Go, retain ownership, stop-path, and changed-case evidence.
